prompt:
ܦܪܫܓܢܐ ܕܬܢܝܢܘܬ ܩܪܝܬܐ

ܩܦܠܐܘܢ ܐ

ܐ:ܐ ܬܢܝܢܘܬ ܩܪܝܬܐ ܗܝ ܥܒܝܕܬܐ ܕܒܐܘܡܢܘܬ ܦܘܩܕ̈ܢܐ ܕܡܬܬܣܝܡܐ܂ ܘܒܗ ܣܕܪܐ ܕܦܘܩܕ̈ܢܐ ܩܪܐ ܠܢܦܫܗ ܒܓܘ ܗܠܟܬܐ ܕܫܘܠܡܗ ܕܝܠܗ܀
(Recursion is a technique that in the craft of commands is established; and in it, a sequence of commands calls itself within the process of its own completion.)

ܐ:ܒ ܗܢܘ ܕܝܢ܂ ܫܪܒܬܐ ܕܦܘܩܕ̈ܢܐ ܕܐܬܬܨܝܪܬ ܠܡܫܠܡ ܥܒܕܐ ܚܕ ܡܬܦܢܝܐ ܘܩܪܝܐ ܠܗ ܠܕܝܠܗ ܡܢ ܓܘ ܬܚܘܡ̈ܝܗ ܕܝܠܗ ܟܕ ܕܝܠܗ܀
(That is to say: a body of commands which was fashioned to accomplish a certain task turns and calls its own self from within its own boundaries, even its very own.)

ܐ:ܓ ܟܠ ܩܪܝܬܐ ܕܗܟܢ ܡܬܥܒܕܐ ܡܘܠܕܐ ܨܘܪܬܐ ܚܕܬܐ ܕܗܝ ܗܝ ܫܪܒܬܐ ܕܦܘܩܕ̈ܢܐ܂ ܘܗܕܐ ܨܘܪܬܐ ܚܕܬܐ ܡܫܬܡܫܐ ܒܡ̈ܠܐ ܕܝܠܢ̈ܝܬܐ ܕܠܗ ܐܬܝ̈ܗܒ܂ ܕܡܫ̈ܚܠܦܢ ܡܢ ܗ̈ܠܝܢ ܕܡܢ ܩܕܡ ܗܟܢ ܐܬܝ̈ܗܒ܀
(Each calling that is thus made begets a new instance of that same body of commands, and this new instance operates upon particular values that were given to it, which differ from those that were given before.)

ܐ:ܕ ܘܒܟܠ ܬܢܝܢܘܬ ܩܪܝܬܐ ܕܗܟܢ ܡܬܥܒܕܐ܂ ܥܒܕܐ ܡܬܦܠܓ ܘܡܬܬܒܪ ܠܡ̈ܢܘܬܐ ܙܥܘܪ̈ܝܬܐ ܡܢ ܗܝ ܕܗܘܬ ܡܢ ܩܕܝܡ܂ ܘܟܠ ܡܢܬܐ ܡܬܡܣܪܐ ܠܩܪܝܬܐ ܐܚܪܬܐ ܕܗܝ ܗܝ ܫܪܒܬܐ ܕܦܘܩܕ̈ܢܐ܀
(And in each recursion that is thus performed, the task is divided and broken into portions smaller than that which was before, and each portion is delivered unto another calling of that same body of commands.)

ܐ:ܗ ܘܐܢܢܩܐ ܗܝ ܘܐܠܨܐ ܕܢܗܘܐ ܬܚܘܡܐ ܩܪܝܪܐ ܕܫܘܠܡܐ ܒܟܠ ܬܢܝܢܘܬ ܩܪܝܬܐ ܕܗܟܢ ܡܬܬܣܝܡܐ܂ ܗܢܘ ܕܝܢ ܬܢܝ ܩܝܡܐ ܕܒܗ ܫܪܒܬܐ ܕܦܘܩܕ̈ܢܐ ܠܐ ܬܘܒ ܩܪܝܐ ܠܢܦܫܗ܂ ܐܠܐ ܡܦܢܝܐ ܦܬܓܡܐ ܒܠܥܕ ܩܪܝܬܐ ܐܚܪܬܐ܀
(And it is necessary and required that there be a fixed boundary of completion in every recursion that is thus established; that is to say, a condition in which the body of commands no longer calls itself, but returns an answer without another calling.)

ܐ:ܘ ܗܢܐ ܬܚܘܡܐ ܕܫܘܠܡܐ ܡܬܩܪܐ ܐܣ̈ܐ ܕܬܢܝܢܘܬ ܩܪܝܬܐ܂ ܘܒܠܥܕܘܗܝ ܫܪܒܬܐ ܕܦܘܩܕ̈ܢܐ ܬܩܪܐ ܠܢܦܫܗ ܠܥܠܡ ܥܠ̈ܡܝܢ ܕܠܐ ܫܘܠܡ܂ ܘܬܬܛܥܢ ܐܘܪܚ ܫܘܡܠܝܐ ܥܕܡܐ ܠܐܒܕܢܐ ܕܕܘܟܪ̈ܢܐ ܕܡ̈ܐܢܝ ܚܘܫܒ̈ܢܐ܀
(This boundary of completion is called the base of the recursion, and without it the body of commands would call itself forever and ever without end, and the path of execution would be burdened unto the destruction of the memory stores of the instruments of computation.)

ܐ:ܙ ܘܡܢ ܗܟܝܠ ܟܕ ܬܚܘܡܐ ܕܫܘܠܡܐ ܡܬܡܛܐ ܒܚܕ ܡܢ ܩܪ̈ܝܬܐ ܗ̈ܠܝܢ ܦܬ̈ܓܡܐ ܫܪܝܢ ܠܡܬܦܢܝܘ ܒܣܕܪ ܗܦܟ̈ܬܐ܂ ܡܢ ܩܪܝܬܐ ܐܚܪܝܬܐ ܥܕܡܐ ܠܩܕܡܝܬܐ܀
(And thus when the boundary of completion is reached in one of the callings, answers begin to be returned in the order of reversal, from the last calling unto the first.)

ܐ:ܚ ܘܟܠ ܩܪܝܬܐ ܡܩܒܠܐ ܦܬܓܡܐ ܡܢ ܗܝ ܕܩܪܬ ܠܗ܂ ܘܡܫܠܡܐ ܡܢ ܗܝ ܡܢ ܥܒܕܐ ܕܝܠܗ ܘܡܦܢܝܐ ܦܬ̈ܓܡܐ ܠܗܝ ܕܩܪܬ ܠܗ ܡܢ ܩܕܝܡ܂ ܥܕܡܐ ܕܟܠ ܩܪ̈ܝܬܐ ܡܫܬܠ̈ܡܢ ܘܦܬܓܡܐ ܫܪܝܪܐ ܕܪܝܫ ܩܪܝܬܐ ܡܬܝܕܥ܀
(And each calling receives an answer from the one which it called, and completes thereby its own task and returns answers to the one that called it before, until all callings are completed and the true answer of the first calling is made known.)

ܐ:ܛ ܬܢܝܢܘܬ ܩܪܝܬܐ ܡܕܝܢ ܗܝ ܥܒܝܕܬܐ ܕܒܗ ܫܘܠܡ ܥܒܕ̈ܐ ܡܬܦܪܫ ܒܝܕ ܫܪܒܬܐ ܕܦܘܩ̈ܕܢܐ ܕܡܫܬܥܒܕ ܠܢܦܫܗ ܒܨܘܪ̈ܬܐ ܡ̈ܬܚܕܬܢܝܬܐ ܥܕܡܐ ܠܬܚܘܡ ܫܘܠܡܐ ܕܡܬܬܣܝܡ ܩܕ̈ܡ ܥܝ̈ܢܝ ܗ̈ܠܟܬ ܦܘܩ̈ܕܢܐ܀
(Recursion then is the technique in which the completion of tasks is accomplished by a body of commands that submits unto itself in renewed instances, until the boundary of completion that is set before the eyes of the processes of commands is reached.)

ܐ:ܝ ܘܒܗ ܬܪ̈ܬܝܢ ܥ̈ܠܠܢ ܐ̈ܠܨܝܬܐ ܐܝ̈ܬܝܗܝܢ ܒܟܠ ܙܒܢ܂ ܚܕܐ ܗܝ ܩܪܝܬ ܢܦܫܐ ܕܒܗ ܫܪܒܬܐ ܕܦܘܩ̈ܕܢܐ ܩܪܝܐ ܠܢܦܫܗ܂ ܘܕܬܪ̈ܬܝܢ ܗܝ ܬܚܘܡ ܫܘܠܡܐ ܕܒܗ ܩܪ̈ܝܬܐ ܫ̈ܠܝܢ ܘܡ̈ܬܦܢܝܢ ܦܬ̈ܓܡܐ ܒܠܥܕ ܩܪܝܬܐ ܐܚܪܬܐ܀
(And in it two necessary elements exist at all times: the first is the self-calling, in which the body of commands calls itself; and the second is the boundary of completion, in which the callings cease and answers are returned without another calling.)

ܐ:ܝܐ ܘܗ̈ܠܝܢ ܬܪ̈ܬܝܗܝܢ ܟܕ ܡ̈ܚܝܕܢ ܥ̈ܒܕܢ ܠܬܢܝܢܘܬ ܩܪܝܬܐ ܕܬܗܘܐ ܫܪܝܪܬܐ ܘܡ̈ܫܡܠܝܬܐ ܒܐܘܡܢܘܬ ܦܘܩ̈ܕܢܐ܀
(And these two, when joined together, cause the recursion to be sound and complete in the craft of commands.)
response:
# ܦܪܫܓܢܐ ܕܬܢܝܢܘܬ ܩܪܝܬܐ

## Translation of the Treatise on Recursion

### Chapter One

**1:1** Recursion is a technique that is established in the craft of programming. In it, a sequence of commands calls itself within the process of its own completion.

**1:2** That is to say: a body of commands which was fashioned to accomplish a certain task turns back and calls its own self from within its own boundaries — even its very own.

**1:3** Each call that is thus made begets a new instance of that same body of commands, and this new instance operates upon particular values that were given to it, which differ from those that were given before.

**1:4** And in each recursion that is thus performed, the task is divided and broken into portions smaller than that which existed before, and each portion is delivered unto another call of that same body of commands.

**1:5** And it is necessary and required that there be a fixed boundary of completion in every recursion that is thus established — that is to say, a condition in which the body of commands no longer calls itself, but returns an answer without another call.

**1:6** This boundary of completion is called the **base case** of the recursion, and without it the body of commands would call itself forever and ever without end, and the path of execution would be burdened unto the destruction of the memory stores of the instruments of computation.

**1:7** And thus, when the boundary of completion is reached in one of the calls, answers begin to be returned in reverse order — from the last call unto the first.

**1:8** And each call receives an answer from the one which it called, and thereby completes its own task and returns answers to the one that called it before, until all calls are completed and the true answer of the first call is made known.

**1:9** Recursion, then, is the technique in which the completion of tasks is accomplished by a body of commands that submits unto itself in renewed instances, until the boundary of completion that is set before the eyes of the processes of commands is reached.

**1:10** And in it, two necessary elements exist at all times: the first is the **self-call**, in which the body of commands calls itself; and the second is the **base case**, in which the calls cease and answers are returned without another call.

**1:11** And these two, when joined together, cause the recursion to be sound and complete in the craft of programming.

---

## Commentary

This is a remarkable text: a complete and technically accurate explanation of **recursion in computer science**, composed in **Classical Syriac** (ܣܘܪܝܝܐ) in the style of patristic or scholastic Syriac prose — structured in numbered verses reminiscent of scriptural or canonical literature.

The key technical concepts are rendered into Syriac as follows:

| Syriac Term | Meaning | CS Concept |
|---|---|---|
| ܬܢܝܢܘܬ ܩܪܝܬܐ | "repetition of calling" | Recursion |
| ܫܪܒܬܐ ܕܦܘܩ̈ܕܢܐ | "body of commands" | Function |
| ܬܚܘܡ ܫܘܠܡܐ / ܐܣ̈ܐ | "boundary of completion" / "base" | Base case |
| ܩܪܝܬ ܢܦܫܐ | "calling of self" | Self-call / recursive call |
| ܨܘܪܬܐ ܚܕܬܐ | "new instance/form" | New stack frame / instance |
| ܡ̈ܠܐ ܕܝܠܢ̈ܝܬܐ | "particular values" | Arguments / parameters |
| ܐܘܪܚ ܫܘܡܠܝܐ | "path of execution" | Execution path |
| ܕܘܟܪ̈ܢܐ ܕܡ̈ܐܢܝ ܚܘܫܒ̈ܢܐ | "memory of instruments of computation" | Computer memory (stack overflow) |
| ܣܕܪ ܗܦܟ̈ܬܐ | "order of reversals" | Unwinding of the call stack |

The text faithfully captures every essential aspect of recursion: self-reference, diminishing subproblems, the base case, the danger of infinite recursion and stack overflow, and the unwinding of the call stack in reverse order. It is, in effect, a page from a computer science textbook written as though it were a chapter of a Syriac Church Father's treatise.
